name: Nasal vowels ą and ę
description: Use the Polish nasal vowel letters; their pronunciation changes before different consonants.

name: Acute-mark consonants
description: Ć, ń, ś, and ź mark soft consonants. Before another vowel the same sounds are commonly written ci, ni, si, and zi.

name: The letter ł
description: Ł is a separate Polish letter and must not be replaced by l.

name: Ó or u
description: Ó and u normally represent the same sound. Word families and endings often reveal the conventional spelling.

name: Rz or ż
description: Rz and ż usually represent the same sound. Alternations such as rz/r can help, but many words must be learned.

name: Ch or h
description: Ch and h are pronounced alike by most speakers. Alternations such as ch/sz can help with some word families.

name: Polish digraphs
description: These letter groups represent single consonants; dź and dż differ in softness and articulation.

name: I and j around vowels
description: I can mark consonant softness and a vowel, while j marks the glide in forms such as moje and kraj.

name: Foreign-noun endings
description: Loanwords vary between -ii, -ji, and -i in inflected forms according to their stem and pronunciation.
